title: Isaiah 26
subtitle: A Song of Trust: The Dead Will Live

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — “You Will Keep in Perfect Peace Those Whose Minds Are Steadfast”

heb.paragraph: "You will keep in perfect peace (shālōm shālōm) those whose minds are steadfast, because they trust in you. Trust in the LORD forever, for the LORD, the LORD himself, is the Rock eternal" (vv.3–4). Shālōm shālōm — doubled for emphasis: peace peace. The condition: a steadfast mind. The cause: trust. The foundation: the eternal Rock. The city of salvation has strong walls; the gates open for the righteous (vv.1–2). The lofty city is brought low to the dust; the feet of the oppressed trample it (v.6). "The path of the righteous is level; you, the Upright One, make the way of the righteous smooth" (v.7). Isaiah’s soul yearns for God in the night (v.9). Even when God’s grace is shown to the wicked, they do not learn righteousness (v.10).

notes.note: "Perfect peace to those whose minds are steadfast, because they trust in you. Trust in the LORD forever, for the LORD is the Rock eternal." Doubled shālōm. The most beloved peace-promise in Isaiah. The Rock eternal: ṣūr ʿōlāmîm.

"The path of the righteous is level. My soul yearns for you in the night; in the morning my spirit longs for you." Night-yearning, morning-longing. The same rhythm as Psalm 63. Desire for God across the hours.
"Though grace is shown to the wicked, they do not learn righteousness." The hardening problem: grace does not automatically produce goodness. Some receive mercy and remain unchanged.

refs.note: 'God is our refuge and strength' — the same trust vocabulary in Zion's strong city.
'The peace of God which transcends all understanding' — Paul's 'perfect peace' echoes Isaiah's shalom.
'My peace I give you' — Jesus grants the peace Isaiah promised to the steadfast mind.
'Peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ' — the NT fulfillment of Isaiah's peace theology.

hist.context: Chapter 26 is a song of trust, likely liturgical, sung by the righteous who enter the 'strong city' (v.1). The contrast with the 'lofty city' brought low (v.5) continues the reversal theme. The famous 'perfect peace' (shalom shalom, v.3) is given to the mind 'stayed on' Yahweh — fixed, steadfast, trusting. This is not mere feeling but settled orientation. The path of the righteous is 'level' (v.7), not because life is easy but because God makes it navigable.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt emphasizes the contrast: the proud city of chapter 24 versus the strong city of chapter 26. One trusts in walls and wealth; the other trusts in Yahweh who is the 'Rock eternal.'

The doubled 'shalom shalom' (perfect peace) is emphatic. Oswalt notes that peace is not absence of trouble but presence of trust. The 'steadfast mind' is one that keeps returning to God despite circumstances.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads this as the community's response to God's victory in chapters 24-25. The song they will sing 'in that day' is already given — the future liturgy shapes present hope.

The tension between the righteous path (v.7) and the wicked who do not learn (v.10) reflects the already/not-yet of prophetic eschatology. God's victory is certain, but some refuse to see it even when grace is shown.

sections.header: Verses 12–21 — “Your Dead Will Live; Their Bodies Will Rise”

heb.paragraph: "But your dead will live (yiḥyū), LORD; their bodies will rise — let those who dwell in the dust wake up and shout for joy — your dew is like the dew of the morning; the earth will give birth to her dead" (v.19). Alongside Daniel 12:2, the OT’s strongest resurrection text. God’s dead LIVE; their bodies RISE; the dust-dwellers WAKE and SHOUT. The dew of morning falls on graves and produces life. The earth in labour, giving birth to the dead. "Go, my people, enter your rooms and shut the doors behind you; hide yourselves for a little while until his wrath has passed by" (v.20) — the Passover-night image: shelter while judgment passes over. Then: "the earth will disclose the blood shed on it; the earth will conceal its slain no longer" (v.21). Every hidden injustice revealed.

notes.note: "LORD, you establish peace for us. We acknowledge no God but you. They are now dead, they live no more." The dead tyrants stay dead. The wicked do not rise.

"Your dead will live; their bodies will rise. Wake up and shout for joy, you who dwell in the dust! Your dew is like the dew of the morning; the earth will give birth to her dead." Resurrection as morning dew: gentle, life-giving, quiet. The earth as a womb delivering the dead into new life.

"Go, my people, enter your rooms and shut the doors behind you. Hide until his wrath has passed. The earth will disclose the blood shed on it." Passover echoes: go inside, shut the door, wait for the destroyer to pass. Then: all hidden blood revealed. No crime stays buried.

refs.note: The dead shall awake — Daniel's explicit resurrection promise may draw on Isaiah 26:19.
The valley of dry bones — Ezekiel's vision of national resurrection uses similar imagery.
'The dead in Christ shall rise' — Paul's resurrection teaching stands in this prophetic tradition.
All in their graves will hear his voice — Jesus' resurrection promise echoes Isaiah's vision.

hist.context: Isaiah 26:19 is one of the earliest Old Testament hints at bodily resurrection: 'Your dead will live; their bodies will rise.' The interpretation is debated — is this national restoration (like Ezekiel 37) or individual resurrection? The Hebrew is ambiguous. By the Second Temple period, this verse became proof-text for resurrection faith. The 'birth pangs' imagery (vv.17-18) suggests Israel's efforts produce nothing; only God's action brings life from death.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt notes the contrast: the oppressors are dead and will not rise (v.14), but 'your dead will live' (v.19). Death is not the final word for God's people. The birth-pang imagery shows human effort's futility.
The 'little while' until indignation passes (v.20) anticipates the NT's 'little while' motif. Oswalt sees a pattern: hiding, waiting, then vindication. The faithful must endure the interval.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs argues this is genuine resurrection hope, not merely metaphor for national restoration. The dew of light falling on the land of shades (Rephaim) reverses death itself. Later Jewish and Christian interpreters were right to find resurrection here.

The 'chambers' where the people hide echo the Passover (Exod 12:22-23). Childs sees typological connection: as Israel was protected during Egypt's judgment, so the faithful are protected during cosmic judgment.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: Trust in the LORD forever, for the LORD is the Rock eternal…

rec.text: ""Perfect peace to those whose minds are steadfast, because they trust in you. Trust in the LORD forever, for the LORD is the Rock eternal." Doubled shālōm. The most beloved peace-promise in Isaiah. Th…"

rows.label: The Dead Will Live — 26:12–21
rows.text: "Your dead will LIVE; their bodies will RISE!" Dew of morning on graves. Earth gives BIRTH to her dead. The Passover shelter.

hebtext.note: "You will keep in perfect peace (shālōm shālōm) those whose minds are steadfast, because they trust in you. Trust in the LORD forever, for the LORD, the LORD himself, is the Rock eternal" (vv.3–4). Shālōm shālōm — doubled for emphasis: peace peace. The condition: a steadfast mind. The cause: trust. The foundation: the eternal Rock. The city of salvation has strong walls; the gates open for the righteous (vv.1–2). The lofty city is brought low to the dust; the feet of the oppressed trample it (v.6). "The path of the righteous is level; you, the Upright One, make the way of the righteous smooth" (v.7). Isaiah’s soul yearns for God in the night (v.9). Even when God’s grace is shown to the wicked, they do not learn righteousness (v.10).

"But your dead will live (yiḥyū), LORD; their bodies will rise — let those who dwell in the dust wake up and shout for joy — your dew is like the dew of the morning; the earth will give birth to her dead" (v.19). Alongside Daniel 12:2, the OT’s strongest resurrection text. God’s dead LIVE; their bodies RISE; the dust-dwellers WAKE and SHOUT. The dew of morning falls on graves and produces life. The earth in labour, giving birth to the dead. "Go, my people, enter your rooms and shut the doors behind you; hide yourselves for a little while until his wrath has passed by" (v.20) — the Passover-night image: shelter while judgment passes over. Then: "the earth will disclose the blood shed on it; the earth will conceal its slain no longer" (v.21). Every hidden injustice revealed.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: resurrection text alongside Daniel 12:2. Earth gives birth to the dead. Faith at 9: perfect peace through trust in the eternal Rock. The Passover-shelter image.

coaching.tip: 'You will keep in perfect peace those whose minds are steadfast, because they trust in you' (v. 3). The Hebrew is shalom shalom — doubled for emphasis. This isn't the absence of conflict but the presence of God. The verse became one of the most memorized in Scripture because it compresses Isaiah's entire theology into one sentence: trust produces peace, and God sustains both.

'Your dead will live; their bodies will rise' (v. 19). This is one of the Old Testament's clearest resurrection texts, alongside Daniel 12:2. Whether Isaiah means national restoration (like Ezekiel 37's dry bones) or individual resurrection, the trajectory is unmistakable — death is not God's final word. The New Testament reads both meanings as ultimately converging in Christ's resurrection.
